reasoning: A "treaty obligation" requires a formally ratified international treaty. As of early 2024, no specific diplomatic agreement or treaty restricting solar geoengineering exists. The diplomatic process — drafting, adoption, signature, and importantly, ratification by at least 10 national parliaments — typically spans many years. Even if a fast-track coalition of countries (like Mexico or Pacific Island nations) initiated such a treaty in 2024/2025, reaching 10 legal ratifications by the end of 2025 would be virtually unprecedented in international environmental law. Additionally, recent UN Environment Assembly meetings (like UNEA-6 in 2024) failed to even reach a consensus on simply studying solar geoengineering, showing how polarized and early-stage the diplomatic conversations are. Therefore, the chance of reaching a binding treaty obligation among 10 states before 2026 is extremely low.
